text: Three-arm relay two is a harder iteration of three-arm relay one in which you are required to open color-coded panels using two arms and with the third arm grab a color-coded shape which is designated for placement in a successive panel. Sticking with the same coordinated movements for each successive panel will build in muscle memory and allow you to navigate this exercise.

The robotic system's left arm grabs the bottom door handle, the furthest right arm holds the top door handle, and the middle arm picks up the color-coded shapes. This arrangement prevents instrument collisions and optimizes the shape-grabbing process.

The robotic arms maneuver to grab the stems of color-coded shapes in a vertical orientation, carefully placing them into designated receptacles within panels that have depth, minimizing force and avoiding collisions.

Obviously coordination with the three arms is key here and we want to emphasize the continuous adjustment with the camera as well as the small movements between the three arms in order to prevent collision and keep everything in the field of view.

The blue piece is placed into its designated receptacle, followed by the green shape being grasped and returned to its original receptacle on the far right. The arms are relayed back to facilitate this movement.

The surgeon swaps the middle and right robotic arms while adjusting the camera multiple times to achieve optimal positioning for safely placing the green piece back into its designated receptacle. The exercise emphasizes using minimal force and small movements to open doors and maintain all three instruments within the camera's field of view.
